title: Doorbell Not Working — Causes & How to Fix It
question: My wired doorbell stopped working — no sound when pressed. What are the likely causes and what can I safely check and fix myself?
summary: A standard wired doorbell is low-voltage and safe to troubleshoot. The four parts that fail are the push button (most common — corrosion or worn contacts), the chime unit, the transformer, or the wiring. Test from the button inward: jump the button wires to see if the chime rings, then check the transformer voltage. Most fixes are a cheap button or chime swap. Note: smart/video doorbells add their own power and Wi-Fi issues.

commonCauses.cause: Worn or corroded push button. The most exposed part — weather and use corrode the contacts so pressing it no longer completes the circuit.
commonCauses.quickCheck: Unscrew the button, remove the two wires, and touch them together briefly. If the chime rings, the button is bad — replace it.

commonCauses.cause: Failed chime unit or stuck plunger. The chime's solenoid plungers stick or the unit dies, so the circuit completes but nothing sounds.
commonCauses.quickCheck: If touching the button wires together does NOT ring the chime, move to the chime: check for dust/stuck plungers, and confirm it gets voltage.

commonCauses.cause: Dead or weak transformer. The small transformer that steps line voltage down to ~16-24V doorbell power can fail, leaving the whole system dead.
commonCauses.quickCheck: With a multimeter on AC volts, check across the transformer's low-voltage terminals (or the chime's 'front'/'trans' terminals). No reading (0V) means a dead transformer or upstream wiring/breaker.

fixes: Start at the button: unscrew it, detach the two wires, and tap them together. A ring means the button is the problem — install a new button.
No ring from the button test? Go to the chime. Remove the cover, vacuum dust, and gently free any stuck plungers.
Check power at the chime with a multimeter (AC volts) across the transformer/front terminals — you should see roughly 16-24V. If present and the chime still won't sound, replace the chime unit.

No voltage at the chime? Find the transformer (often near the panel, in the basement, or in a closet/attic) and measure its low-voltage output. 0V means a dead transformer (replace it) or a tripped breaker / broken wire feeding it.
If the transformer reads correctly but a wire run is broken inside walls, running new low-voltage wire or going to a wireless/smart doorbell is the practical fix.

For smart/video doorbells: confirm the transformer meets the device's voltage requirement, the breaker is on, and Wi-Fi/app connectivity is working — these add power and network failure points a basic chime doesn't have.
diyVsPro: DIY-friendly — standard doorbells run on safe low voltage, and button/chime swaps need only a screwdriver and a multimeter. Call an electrician if the transformer is dead and hardwired into the panel area and you're not comfortable replacing it, if you find scorched wiring, if a smart doorbell needs a higher-voltage transformer or a new circuit, or if there's no low-voltage wiring and you'd rather not fish new wire. The line-voltage side of the transformer is not a beginner job.
typicalCostUSD: $10-$30 for a new button or chime; $15-$40 for a transformer. A multimeter is $15-$40 if you don't have one. An electrician to replace a hardwired transformer runs $100-$250.
timeEstimate: 20-45 minutes to test and replace a button or chime.
safetyNote: The doorbell's low-voltage side (button, chime wiring) is safe to handle, but the transformer's line-voltage side is full household voltage — shut off that circuit at the breaker before touching the transformer's high-voltage wiring, and confirm it's dead. If you find scorched or melted wiring anywhere, stop and call an electrician. Don't poke a multimeter into line-voltage terminals unless you know what you're doing.
toolsParts: Replacement push button and/or chime unit
Small vacuum or brush (to clean the chime)
searchTerms: how to test a doorbell button
ring doorbell not getting power transformer
sources: General low-voltage doorbell wiring and troubleshooting guidance (button/chime/transformer)
Smart-doorbell manufacturer (Ring, Nest) transformer voltage requirements and wiring guidance
Electrical-safety guidance on line-voltage transformer connections
disclaimer: General guidance only. The transformer's line-voltage side carries household current; if you are not comfortable working near it or find damaged wiring, consult a licensed electrician.
verifierNote: Drafted from standard low-voltage doorbell practice (button-jump test, voltage check at chime/transformer). Pending human source verification.
